stem: A population is modeled by $P(t) = P_0 \cdot b^t$, where $t$ is time in years and $P_0$ and $b$ are positive constants. At time $t = 2$, the population is $20$. At time $t = 5$, the population is $160$. What is the population at time $t = 8$?
explanation:
Step 1: Set up equations using the two given data points.
$$P(2) = P_0 \cdot b^2 = 20 \quad \text{and} \quad P(5) = P_0 \cdot b^5 = 160$$

Step 2: Divide the second equation by the first to eliminate $P_0$.
$$\frac{P_0 \cdot b^5}{P_0 \cdot b^2} = \frac{160}{20} \implies b^3 = 8 \implies b = 2$$

Step 3: Solve for $P_0$ using $P(2) = 20$.
$$P_0 \cdot 2^2 = 20 \implies P_0 \cdot 4 = 20 \implies P_0 = 5$$

Step 4: Compute $P(8)$.
$$P(8) = 5 \cdot 2^8 = 5 \cdot 256 = 1{,}280$$

Verification: $P(2) = 5 \cdot 4 = 20$ ✓ and $P(5) = 5 \cdot 32 = 160$ ✓.

distractorRationale.A: Error: The student correctly finds $b = 2$ and $P_0 = 5$, but then computes $P(8) = 5 \cdot 2^6 = 5 \cdot 64 = 320$. The single error is using $6$ as the exponent (the number of years from $t = 2$ to $t = 8$) instead of $8$ (the actual value of $t$). The correct formula requires substituting $t = 8$ directly: $P_0 \cdot b^8$.
distractorRationale.B: Error: The student correctly finds $b = 2$ and $P_0 = 5$, but evaluates $P(8) = 5 \cdot 2^7 = 5 \cdot 128 = 640$, using exponent $7$ instead of $8$. This is an off-by-one error in writing the exponent when substituting $t = 8$.
distractorRationale.D: Error: The student correctly finds $b^3 = 8$ so $b = 2$, but makes a single error when solving for $P_0$: divides $P(2)$ by $b$ instead of $b^2$, writing $P_0 = 20 \div 2 = 10$. Then $P(8) = 10 \cdot 2^8 = 10 \cdot 256 = 2{,}560$.
